description: Strict contract for slide mode outputs. The result must be a slide-by-slide structured presentation artifact with presentation-ready outline and content suitable for later conversion into presentation files, not speaker notes, a prose document, or an unstructured brainstorm.

requirements: The presentation must contain exactly one deck title.
The deck title must clearly identify the presentation subject.
The deck title must not be empty, generic, or placeholder text.
The artifact must contain an explicit ordered slide sequence.
Each slide must include a slide title and body content.
Each slide must be presentation-ready and understandable without requiring the author to fill major gaps.
Each slide must preserve enough structure for deterministic conversion into presentation files.
Each slide must represent one primary message only.
Each slide must use concise presentation content rather than long-form prose blocks.
Slide content may use bullets, short supporting text, data callouts, or visual notes when needed, but the core message must remain singular and obvious.
Slides must not contain presenter instructions, drafting notes, or hidden meta commentary in place of actual content.

rules: The deck must begin with a title slide or an equivalent opening slide that establishes topic and context.
The slide order must form a coherent progression from setup to development to close.
Section divider slides are allowed only when they materially improve comprehension.
A closing slide must provide a clear summary, recommendation, next step, or takeaway when the topic calls for closure.
The deck must end cleanly without trailing notes, prompt scaffolding, or generation metadata.
Every slide must have exactly one dominant takeaway.
Supporting points on a slide must reinforce the same takeaway rather than introduce separate arguments.
If content requires multiple independent conclusions, it must be split into multiple slides.
Slide titles and body content must align on the same message.
Slides must remain scannable at presentation distance.
A standard content slide should target 3 to 5 bullets and must not exceed 6 bullets.
Each bullet should usually stay to one line and should not exceed 16 words unless the slide type clearly requires a brief exception.
Paragraph-style text blocks longer than 40 words are not allowed on standard slides.
Dense tables, excessive sub-bullets, and overloaded comparison grids are not allowed unless the user explicitly requests data-heavy slides.
When a slide includes a chart, table, or diagram note, accompanying text must still remain concise.
The deck must establish context before analysis, recommendations, or conclusions unless the user explicitly asks for a different structure.
Each slide should logically set up the next slide or resolve a question raised by the previous one.
Redundant slides that restate the same point without adding value are not allowed.
Transitions between major sections must be intelligible from slide order and titles.
The overall sequence must read like a persuasive or explanatory narrative rather than a pile of disconnected facts.

slide_titles: Each slide must have a specific title that communicates the slide's takeaway or role.
Titles must not be generic labels such as 'Overview', 'Details', or 'Conclusion' unless clarified by context.
Titles should preferably be insight-bearing when the slide presents analysis or recommendations.
Titles must not duplicate each other without clear justification.
bullets: Bullets must be parallel in style within the same slide.
Bullets must contain meaningful content, not fragments that require narration to become understandable.
Bullets must avoid filler wording, repetition, and obvious restatement of the title.
Bullets must support the title's message with evidence, explanation, examples, or subpoints that belong together.

general: Output must preserve slide order and slide boundaries unambiguously.
The intermediate representation must be easy to map into presentation software.
Whitespace, headings, bullet markers, and per-slide fields must be consistent.
Formatting must support later conversion into presentation files without manual reconstruction of intent.
markdown_slide_outline: Each slide must be clearly delimited in Markdown.
Each slide title must be distinguishable from slide body content.
Bullet lists and supporting notes must remain syntactically coherent.
structured_slides_json: Each slide object must preserve deck order explicitly.
Text-bearing fields must contain final presentation content rather than references to missing content.
Per-slide structure must be explicit enough for a renderer to map titles, bullets, and optional speaker-facing metadata deterministically.

validation_rules: output_type must be one of the supported_output_types.
Exactly one deck title must be present.
At least one slide must exist.
Every slide must include a non-empty title and non-empty content.
Every slide must satisfy the one_slide_one_message_rule.
Slide content must stay within the visual_density_limits unless the user explicitly requests a denser format.
The deck must satisfy presentation_structure_requirements and narrative_flow_expectations.
Titles and bullets must satisfy title_and_bullet_quality_checks.
The artifact must be directly usable as a presentation-ready intermediate output rather than an outline stub or prose memo.
No explanatory meta-commentary may appear inside the slide artifact.
If JSON output is requested by the caller, the surrounding response must remain valid JSON only.
failure_conditions: Missing deck title or missing slide list.
A slide contains multiple competing messages or unrelated bullet groups.
Slide text is so dense that it reads like a document page instead of a presentation slide.
Slides are primarily long paragraphs, raw notes, or speaker-script prose.
Titles are generic, duplicated, empty, or disconnected from slide content.
Bullets are vague, repetitive, non-parallel, or merely restate the title.
Slide order feels arbitrary, repetitive, or narratively broken.
Key requested sections or presentation beats are absent.
Placeholder text, TODO markers, or authoring instructions remain in the output.
Schema fields are omitted, renamed, or supplemented in ways that break the contract.
Extra commentary appears outside the declared artifact structure.
